nativeRange.habitat: Steep outer reef slopes and shallow coastal reef flats, typically found in large aggregations feeding on zooplankton several meters above the substrate at depths of 0–40 m.

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding primarily on zooplankton in mid-water aggregations above reef slopes. In captivity, generally accepts a varied diet including vitamin-enriched flake foods, herbivore preparations, frozen mysis and brine shrimp, and small pellets. Feeding multiple small portions daily is commonly recommended; vitamin-enriched foods are often suggested to help preserve coloration.

summary: A semi-aggressive Indo-Pacific damselfish with an iridescent blue body fading into a pale yellow tail. Generally considered reef-safe and beginner-friendly, though larger and more territorial than the closely-related Blue/Green Chromis.
careNotes:
Generally advised to keep in groups of at least 6 to diffuse conspecific aggression; smaller groups tend to develop hierarchies in which dominant individuals harass subordinates until only one or two remain. Reaches a larger adult size than most aquarium chromis — wild specimens have been reported up to 17 cm TL, with captive individuals typically settling in the 12–15 cm range. The schooling behavior, larger adult size, and territorial tendencies justify a longer tank footprint than the LiveAquaria-listed 50-gallon (~190 L) minimum; 200 L with ample open swimming space is used here.

Temperature note: LiveAquaria recommends 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), while FishBase wild data suggests 24.7–28.9°C; the averaged range of 23.5–27.3°C is used here.

Thrives in reef aquariums with branching corals and live rock that mimic its natural habitat. Vitamin-enriched foods are commonly recommended to preserve the iridescent body coloration.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with parental care typical of damselfishes: males prepare and guard demersal nests on rubble or rockwork until hatching. Rarely bred in home aquaria; spawning has been reported in large systems but rearing the planktonic larvae is generally considered difficult.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care level, temperament, reef compatibility, water parameters, retail tank-size minimum (50 gal), lifespan, and diet notes.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy confirmation, native range/distribution (Western Pacific: Japan to Australia to Tonga), depth range (0–40 m), wild temperature data (24.7–28.9°C), and max size (17 cm TL). FishBase max size larger than LiveAquaria's 6 in (~15.2 cm); used as upper bound of adultSizeCm.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterers with parental care; males guard demersal nests but become aggressive toward conspecifics during spawning. Rarely bred successfully in home aquaria.
